headers.heading:
Asymptotic analysis
class_Link:
  - "[[3 - Algoritmer og datastrukturer (DAT200)]]"
Dataview:
  - "[[UiS/Class Notes/2 - Datateknologi Bachelor 2024-2027/3. Semester/3 - Algoritmer og datastrukturer (DAT200)/DAT200 - Stikkordliste]]"
description: Asymptotic analysis is a method of describing the **efficiency** of algorithms in terms of **input size (n)** as it grows towards infinity. It focuses on the **growth rate** of an algorithm’s time or space complexity, ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms.
example:
tags:
  - course/DAT200/major
  - topic/algorithms/analysis
translation: Asymptotisk analyse

Definition

Asymptotic analysis is a method of describing the efficiency of algorithms in terms of input size (n) as it grows towards infinity. It focuses on the growth rate of an algorithm’s time or space complexity, ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms.

Why it matters

It allows you to compare algorithms independently of hardware or implementation details. By understanding how an algorithm scales, you can choose the most efficient one for large inputs and optimize performance.

Examples

Use case

Common Notations

Related concepts

Tag suggestions

#algorithms #complexity #asymptotic #bigO #analysis

Old

Means looking at an algorithms resources

Big-O Notation
class_Link:
  - "[[3 - Algoritmer og datastrukturer (DAT200)]]"
Dataview:
  - "[[UiS/Class Notes/2 - Datateknologi Bachelor 2024-2027/3. Semester/3 - Algoritmer og datastrukturer (DAT200)/DAT200 - Stikkordliste]]"
description: Notation methods to show algorithms efficiency
example: O(n) means linear growth rate
tags:
  - course/DAT200
  - topic/algorithms/analysis
translation: Stor-O Notasjon

Big-O notation represents the upper limit of an algorithm's cost and is associated with a big O(n) where n is the size of the input

Big-O complexity chart - Most used

Resources
Time complexity - Big-O

How much time an algorithm takes to complete as a function of the size of the input.
Big-O notations is a way to measure time complexity in worst case senario

Space complexity - Big-O

How much memory an algorithm needs to complete its task.
Also uses Big-O notation

Growth rate

How the runtime or resource usage increases relative to the size of the input
Searching an array increases its growth rate as the array grows.

Dominant term

Refers to the term in a mathematical expression that has the highest impact on the overall growth rate of that expression as the input size increases.

Example Table

Operation Big-O Efficiency
indexx[] O(1)
index assignment O(1)
append O(1)
pop() O(1)
pop(i) O(n)
insert(i,item) O(n)
del operator O(n)
iteration O(n)
contains (in) O(n)
get slice [x:y] O(k)
set slice O(n+k)
reverse O(n)
concatenate O(k)
sort O(nlog\ n)
multiply O(nk)
- It refers to a mathematical function applied in computer science to analyze an algorithms complextityIt defines the runtime required for executing an algorithmImportant distinction is tat when talking about asymptotic analyssis, we're ignoring values that don't change the overall shape of the curveO(n+1) would still be expresed as O(n) because it's only the n, the variable component that's influencing the change in behaviour as the limit is approached.
  • O(2n) == O(n) - Same growth rate
- Constant: O(1)
  • Same amount of time regardless of input size. Retrieving a value in array based on index. Array[5]
  • Growth rate chart
Logarithmic time: O(log n)
  • Time to complete reaches a limit as the input size increases. Halveringsmetoden for eksempel
  • Growth rate chart
Linear time: O(n)
  • Time to complete increases linearly based on input size. For loops for example looping through an array.
  • Growth rate chart
Quadratic time: O()
  • Time to complete increases rapidly based on input size. Nested loops for example
  • growth rate chart
Exponential time: O()- Operations - Number of times we need to perform some operationsMemory - How much memory is usedOthers - Network transfer, compression rations, disk usage.
- Linear time: O(n)O(n) — e.g., iterating through an arrayQuadratic time: O(n2)O(n2) — e.g., nested loops for comparing all pairsLogarithmic time: O(log⁡n)O(logn) — e.g., binary searchConstant time: O(1)O(1) — e.g., accessing an array element by index- Algorithm selection based on input sizePerformance prediction for large datasetsBenchmarking and optimizationUnderstanding trade-offs between time and space- Big O (O): Worst-case scenarioBig Ω (Omega): Best-case scenarioBig Θ (Theta): Average or tight boundLittle o / ω: Strictly less/greater growth rate- Time complexitySpace complexityBig-O NotationAlgorithm efficiencyComputational complexity theory- O (Big O): Describes the upper bound (worst-case) - Most used (Big Omega): Describes the lower bound (best-case) (Big Theta): Describes the exact bound (Average-case)

Big-O Notation

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/algorithms.ht.title: Algorithms
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/algorithms.ht.description: An algorithm is a **step-by-step procedure** or set of rules designed to perform a specific task or solve a problem. It takes input, processes it through a sequence of operations, and produces output.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/arrays.html.title: Arrays
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/arrays.html.description: An array is a data structure that stores a fixed-size sequence of elements of the same type, arranged in contiguous memory locations. Each element can be accessed directly via its index.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/asymptotic-an.title: Asymptotic analysis
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/asymptotic-an.description: Asymptotic analysis is a method of describing the **efficiency** of algorithms in terms of **input size (n)** as it grows towards infinity. It focuses on the **growth rate** of an algorithm’s time or space complexity, ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/avl-trees.htm.title: AVL trees
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/avl-trees.htm.description: Definition: An AVL tree is a self-balancing binary search tree (BST) where the height difference (balance factor) between the left and right subtrees of any node is at most 1. Binary Search Tree Rules: Left child < parent < right child. Balance Factor: Number of levels left side - number of levels right side from node of consideration. (Count edges going furthest down) Must be -1, 0, or +1 for every node. This means A positive balance factor indicate left heavy A negative balance factor indicate right heavy Height: Height of an AVL tree with n nodes is O(log⁡n) Insertion: Insert like a normal BST. Update heights and balance factors. If imbalance occurs, perform rotations. Deletion: Delete like a BST. Rebalance using rotations. Search: Same as BST: O(log⁡n) After insertion or deletion, check the balance factor of affected nodes beginning from the bottom. When a balance factor is outside -1<factor<1 you perform considering that node. If node is left heavy (positive), then do right rotation If node is right heavy (negative), then do left rotation. However if the child being rotated up is opposite heavy, then rotate that child in the opposite direction. Note: Do not consider that childs child heavy direction. Just trust the process. Insertion: O(log⁡n) Deletion: O(log⁡n) Search: O(log⁡n) Guaranteed O(log⁡n) operations. Good for applications requiring frequent insertions and deletions. Slightly more complex than regular BST due to rotations. More overhead for maintaining balance. Databases Memory management Real-time systems where predictable performance is critical.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/big-o-notatio.title: Big-O Notation
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/big-o-notatio.description: Notation methods to show algorithms efficiency

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/binary-search.title: Binary search trees
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/binary-search.description: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a type of binary tree where each node has at most two children, and the left child contains values **less than** the parent node, while the right child contains values **greater than** the parent node. This property enables efficient searching, insertion, and deletion

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/breadth-first.title: Breadth-First-Search (BFS)
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/breadth-first.description:
Definition: Breadth-First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all vertices at the current depth level before moving to the next level. It is widely used for finding the shortest path in unweighted graphs and for level-order exploration. Start from a given start vertex. Visit all its adjacent vertices (neighbors). Then visit the neighbors of those vertices, and so on. Ensures that vertices are visited in increasing order of their distance from the start vertex. Traversal Order: Level by level. Data Structure Used: Queue (FIFO). Marking: Vertices should be marked as visited when enqueued to avoid duplicates. Complexity: Time: for adjacency list representation. Space: for storing visited nodes and queue. Initialize a queue and mark the start vertex as visited. Enqueue the start vertex. (Add element to end of array) While the queue is not empty: Dequeue a vertex. (Remove element from front of array) Process it (e.g., print or record). Enqueue all its unvisited adjacent vertices and mark them visited. Repeat until all reachable vertices are visited. Shortest Path in Unweighted Graphs: BFS guarantees the shortest path because it explores vertices in order of their distance from the source. Connectivity Check: Determine if all vertices are reachable. Level Order Traversal: Useful in trees and layered structures. Network Broadcasting: Simulates spreading information level by level. BFS is a fundamental graph traversal method alongside Depth-First-Search (DFS).
BFS is used in Dijkstra's Algorithm for graphs with equal weights.
BFS operates on Graphs represented as adjacency lists or matrices.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/bubble-sort.h.title: Bubble Sort
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/bubble-sort.h.description: Definition: A simple comparison-based sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. Key Idea: Larger elements "bubble up" to the end of the list with each pass. Algorithm Steps: Iterate through the list multiple times. Compare each pair of adjacent elements. Swap if the first is greater than the second. Repeat until no swaps are needed. Complexity: Time: Best case: (already sorted) Average case: Worst case: Stability: Yes, does not change the relative order of equal elements Use Case: Educational purposes; rarely used in practice due to inefficiency. Iterative Solutiondef bubbleSort(arr): for i in range(len(arr) - 1): # loop over array elements for j in range(0, len(arr) - i - 1): # loop to compare array elements # compare two adjacent elements, # Last i elements are already in place, so we don't need to check them if arr[j] > arr[j + 1]: # swap if elements are out-of-order temp = arr[j] arr[j] = arr[j + 1] arr[j + 1] = temp print('pass number', i, arr) arr = [9, 7, 8, 2] print("Original array:", arr) bubbleSort(arr) print("Sorted array:", arr) Recursive Solutiondef recursive_bubble_sort(arr, n): # Base case: If the list has only one element or is empty, it is already sorted. if n <= 1: return # Traverse through all elements in the list for i in range(n - 1): # Swap if the element found is greater than the next element if arr[i] > arr[i + 1]: arr[i], arr[i + 1] = arr[i + 1], arr[i] # Recursively call the function with a reduced size of the list recursive_bubble_sort(arr, n - 1) arr = [9, 7, 8, 2] print("Original array:", arr) recursive_bubble_sort(arr, 4) print("Sorted array:", arr) The largest number in the list will bubble up to the top. Then that number is sorted. No need to check it again. "1 pass" of the algorithm is when the bubble has reached the end.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/circular-link.title: Circular Linked List
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/circular-link.description:
A Circular Linked List is a variation of a linked list where the last node points back to the first node, forming a loop. This structure can be implemented as either:
Circular Singly Linked List: Each node has one pointer (next), and the last node points to the head.
Circular Doubly Linked List: Each node has two pointers (next and previous), and the last node’s next points to the head while the head’s previous points to the last node. Node Components: Data: Stores the value. Pointer(s): One or two pointers depending on singly or doubly implementation. Head: Reference to the first node. Tail: Points back to head to complete the circle. Circular Traversal: No null termination; traversal continues in a loop. Dynamic Size: Can grow or shrink during runtime. Efficient Rotation: Useful for applications like round-robin scheduling. Traversal (O(n)): Start at head and continue until you return to head. Insertion: At beginning: O(1) At end: O(1) if tail is maintained, else O(n) Deletion: From beginning: O(1) From end: O(1) if tail is maintained, else O(n) Searching: O(n) No null pointers; continuous loop. Efficient for cyclic operations. More complex termination conditions during traversal. Slightly harder to implement than linear lists. class Node: def __init__(self, data): self.data = data self.next = None class CircularLinkedList: def __init__(self): self.head = None def insert_end(self, new): new_node = Node(new) if not self.head: self.head = new_node new_node.next = self.head else: temp = self.head while temp.next != self.head: temp = temp.next temp.next = new_node new_node.next = self.head class Node: def __init__(self, data): self.data = data self.next = None self.prev = None class CircularDoublyLinkedList: def __init__(self): self.head = None def insert_end(self, new): new_node = Node(new) if not self.head: # First node points to itself in both directions self.head = new_node new_node.next = self.head new_node.prev = self.head else: tail = self.head.prev # Last node before head tail.next = new_node new_node.prev = tail new_node.next = self.head self.head.prev = new_node def traverse_forward(self): if not self.head: return temp = self.head while True: print(temp.data, end=" ") temp = temp.next if temp == self.head: break print() def traverse_backward(self): if not self.head: return temp = self.head.prev # Start from tail while True: print(temp.data, end=" ") temp = temp.prev if temp == self.head.prev: break print() # Example usage: cdll = CircularDoublyLinkedList() cdll.insert_end(10) cdll.insert_end(20) cdll.insert_end(30) print("Forward Traversal:") cdll.traverse_forward() print("Backward Traversal:") cdll.traverse_backward() RunLinks to Related Notes:
Linked List
Singly Linked List
Doubly Linked List

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/circular-queu.title: Circular Queue
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/circular-queu.description: A queue is a linear data structure that follows the **First In, First Out (FIFO)** principle. Elements are added at the rear (enqueue) and removed from the front (dequeue).

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/data-structur.title: Data structures
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/data-structur.description: A data structure is a specialized format for organizing, storing, and managing data in a computer so it can be accessed and modified efficiently. Different data structures are suited to different kinds of applications and tasks.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/depth-first-s.title: Depth-First-Search (DFS)
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/depth-first-s.description:
Definition: Depth-First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It is commonly used for pathfinding, connectivity checks, and cycle detection in graphs. Start from a given start vertex. Move to an adjacent unvisited vertex and continue exploring deeper. When no unvisited adjacent vertices remain, backtrack to the previous vertex and continue. Repeat until all reachable vertices are visited. Traversal Order: Deep before wide. Data Structure Used: Stack (explicit or via recursion). Complexity: Time: for adjacency list representation. Space: for visited nodes and recursion stack. Initialize a stack (or use recursion) and mark the start vertex as visited. Push the start vertex onto the stack. (Push means add element to the end of the array) While the stack is not empty: Pop a vertex. (Pop means remove the last element in the array) Process it (e.g., print or record). Push all its unvisited adjacent vertices onto the stack and mark them visited. Repeat until all reachable vertices are visited. Pathfinding: Determine if a path exists between two vertices. Cycle Detection: Identify cycles in directed or undirected graphs. Topological Sorting: For Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs). Connected Components: Find components in undirected graphs. DFS complements Breadth-First-Search (BFS) in graph traversal strategies.
Both operate on Graphs represented as adjacency lists or matrices. DFS is foundational for algorithms like Tarjan’s for strongly connected components.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/dijkstra’s-al.title: Dijkstra’s Algorithm
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/dijkstra’s-al.description:
Definition: Dijkstra’s Algorithm is a greedy algorithm used to find the shortest path from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. Unlike a Minimum Spanning Tree, it focuses on minimizing the total distance from the source to each vertex, not connecting all vertices. Start from the source vertex. Assign initial distances: Source = 0 All others = ∞ (infinity). Iteratively select the unvisited vertex with the smallest known distance. Update the distances of its adjacent vertices if a shorter path is found. Repeat until all vertices are visited. Input: Weighted graph (non-negative weights). Output: Shortest path distances from source to all vertices. Approach: Greedy (chooses next closest vertex). Complexity: Using adjacency matrix: . Using min-priority queue (binary heap): . Initialize distances: source = 0, others = ∞. Mark all vertices as unvisited. Select the unvisited vertex with the smallest distance. For each adjacent vertex: Calculate new distance = current distance + edge weight. If new distance < current stored distance, update it. Mark the current vertex as visited. Repeat from step 3 until all vertices are visited. GPS navigation systems. Network routing protocols. Game development (pathfinding). Resource allocation in logistics. Operates on Graphs and uses similar principles to Breadth-First-Search (BFS) for unweighted graphs.
Differs from Prim's Algorithm and Kruskal’s Algorithm (which build MSTs). BFS can be considered a special case of Dijkstra when all edge weights are equal.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/doubly-linked.title: Doubly Linked List
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/doubly-linked.description:
Definition: A Doubly Linked List is a linked list where each node contains data and two pointers: Next: Points to the next node. Previous: Points to the previous node. This structure allows traversal in both directions. Node Components: Data: Stores the value. Next Pointer: Points to the next node. Previous Pointer: Points to the previous node. Head: Reference to the first node. Bidirectional Traversal: Can move forward and backward. Dynamic Size: Can grow or shrink during runtime. No Random Access: Must traverse sequentially. Traversal (O(n)): Forward from head or backward from tail. Insertion: At beginning: O(1) At position: O(n) Deletion: From beginning: O(1) At position: O(n) Searching: O(n) Easier deletion and insertion at both ends. Bidirectional navigation. Extra memory for two pointers per node. Slightly more complex implementation. class Node: def __init__(self, data): self.data = data self.next = None self.prev = None class DoublyLinkedList: def __init__(self): self.head = None def insert_front(self, new): new_node = Node(new) new_node.next = self.head if self.head: self.head.prev = new_node self.head = new_node def traverse_forward(self): temp = self.head while temp: print(temp.data, end=" ") temp = temp.next RunLinks to Related Notes:
Linked List
Singly Linked List
Circular Linked List

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/graphs.html.title: Graphs
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/graphs.html.description:
Definition: A graph is a data structure consisting of: A set of vertices (nodes). A set of edges that connect pairs of vertices, representing relationships between them. Formally: G=(V,E) Where: V(G): finite, non-empty set of vertices. E(G): set of edges (pairs of vertices). Undirected Graph: Edges have no direction. Directed Graph (Digraph): Edges have direction; order of vertices matters. Weighted Graph: Each edge carries a weight or cost. Complete Graph: Every vertex is connected to every other vertex. Edges in complete directed graph: . Edges in complete undirected graph: ​. Trees: Special case of graphs with no cycles. Adjacent Nodes: Two nodes connected by an edge. Path: Sequence of vertices connecting two nodes. Connected Graph: There is a path between every pair of vertices. Strongly Connected Component: In a directed graph, every vertex is reachable from every other vertex. Spanning Tree: A subgraph of an undirected connected graph that includes all vertices with n−1n-1n−1 edges and no cycles. Minimum Spanning Tree (MST): A spanning tree with the minimum possible sum of edge weights. Found using Prim's Algorithm and Kruskal's Algorithm. Adjacency Matrix: Good for dense graphs. Memory: . Fast connectivity checks. Adjacency List: Good for sparse graphs. Memory: . Efficient for finding adjacent vertices. Traversal:
Depth-First-Search (DFS): Explore as far as possible along a branch before backtracking.
Breadth-First-Search (BFS): Explore all neighbors at the current depth before going deeper. Shortest Path:
Dijkstra’s Algorithm for weighted graphs. BFS for unweighted graphs. Networking (routing). Social networks (connections). Pathfinding (maps, games). Dependency resolution.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/hash-function.title: Hash functions
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/hash-function.description: A hash function is a mathematical function that takes an input (or "key") and returns a fixed-size string or number, typically used as an index in a hash table. The output is called a **hash code** or **hash value**.

headers.heading: Properties of a Good Hash Function

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/hash-tables.h.title: Hash tables
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/hash-tables.h.description: A hash table is a data structure that maps **keys to values** using a **hash function**. The hash function computes an index (or hash code) from the key, which determines where the value is stored in memory. This allows for **average-case constant time** complexity for insertion, deletion, and lookup.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/hashing.html.title: Hashing
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/hashing.html.description:
Definition:
Hashing is a technique used to map a large set of possible keys into a smaller range of indices in an array (called a hash table) using a mathematical function known as a hash function. It enables efficient storage and retrieval of data in associative arrays (dictionaries).
Associative Array / Dictionary Stores data as (key, value) pairs.
Keys are unique, case-sensitive; values can be duplicated and of any data type.
Basic operations: Insertion, Deletion, Lookup. Why Hashing? Direct addressing is impractical for large key spaces due to memory inefficiency.
Hashing reduces memory usage by mapping keys to a smaller index range. A data structure combining an array and a hash function.
Each key is transformed into an index using the hash function.
Ideal performance: O(1) for insert, search, and delete (average case). Maps a key to an integer in the range [0, m-1], where m = table size.
Common methods: Division Method: Use a prime number for m to reduce collisions.
Multiplication Method: Where A is a constant (Knuth suggests ).
Universal Hashing:
Randomly selects a hash function from a family to minimize collisions. Occur when two keys hash to the same index.
Collision Resolution Techniques: Probing Chaining:
Each slot stores a linked list of entries. Insert: O(1) at head. Linked list where new element is added to the front. Search/Delete: O(length of list). Open Addressing:
Store colliding keys in other slots using probing: Linear Probing: Check next slot sequentially. Quadratic Probing: Use quadratic increments. Double Hashing: Use a second hash function for step size. Indicates how full the table is.
High load factor → more collisions → degraded performance.
Common threshold: 0.6–0.8; resizing (rehashing) recommended when exceeded. Under Simple Uniform Hashing assumption (keys distributed uniformly): Average lookup cost ≈ O(1). Worst case: O(n) if all keys collide into one slot. Choose a good hash function to minimize collisions.
Resize table when load factor grows too high.
Prime table sizes often improve distribution.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/heap-sort.htm.title: Heap Sort
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/heap-sort.htm.description: Heap Sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that uses a binary heap data structure. Build a max-heap (or min-heap for descending order) from the input array. Repeatedly extract the largest element (root of the heap) and place it at the end of the array. Heapify the array to create a max-heap/min heap. Swap the root with the last element. Now consider the array from index 0 to index n-1 (then n-2 etc.) Min heap, get descending order Max heap, get ascending order. Reversed with initialized array Initialize an array with size n append to new array. Keep order Append to end of array Repeat until the array is sorted. Time: Building heap: O(n) Sorting: O(nlog⁡n) Overall: O(nlog⁡n) Space: O(1)(in-place) Properties: Not stable. Efficient for large datasets. Use Cases: When constant space and predictable performance are required.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/heaps.html.title: Heaps
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/heaps.html.description:
Definition:
A heap is a complete binary tree represented as an array, where each node satisfies the heap property:
Max-Heap: Every parent node is greater than or equal to its children.
Min-Heap: Every parent node is less than or equal to its children.
Heaps are primarily used for efficient priority queue operations (insert and extract), and they form the basis for heap sort.To transform an array into a heap:
Interpret the array as a complete binary tree.
Start from the last parent node (index ⌊n/2⌋ - 1).
Apply heapify down to each parent node, moving backward to the root.
When the root is heapified, the array satisfies the heap property.
Time Complexity:
Bottom-up heapify: O(n)
Inserting elements one by one: O(n log n)
Used when:
Heapifying an array
Building a heap from an array.
Removing the root node (e.g., extract-max or extract-min).
Process:
Compare the parent with its children.
For max-heap, swap with the larger child if the parent is smaller.
For min-heap, swap with the smaller child if the parent is larger.
Repeat until the node is in the correct position or has no children.
Used when:
Adding a new element to the heap.
Process:
Compare the child with its parent.
For max-heap, swap if the child is greater.
For min-heap, swap if the child is smaller.
Repeat until the node is in the correct position or becomes the root.
Time Complexity:
O(log n) n is size of array after adding the new element.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/huffman-codin.title: Huffman Coding
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/huffman-codin.description: Huffman Coding is a lossless data compression algorithm that assigns variable-length binary codes to characters based on their frequencies. Characters that occur more frequently get shorter codes, reducing the overall size of the encoded message. Fixed-length coding (e.g., ASCII) uses the same number of bits for every character, which is inefficient when character frequencies vary. Huffman Coding minimizes the total number of bits by exploiting frequency distribution. Applications: file compression, message transmission, cryptography. Prefix-free codes: No code is a prefix of another, ensuring unique decodability. Optimality: Produces the minimum external path weight for a given set of character frequencies. Lossless: Original message can be perfectly reconstructed. Also see Visual Frequency Analysis: Count occurrences of each character. Priority Queue: Insert all characters as nodes with their frequencies. Tree Construction: Extract two nodes with the smallest frequencies. Create a new internal node with frequency = sum of the two. Insert the new node back into the queue. Repeat until one node remains (the root). Assign Codes: Traverse the tree: Left edge → 0 Right edge → 1 Concatenate path bits for each leaf node. Characters: {E,T,N,I,S} with frequencies {29,10,9,5,4} Steps: Combine smallest: 4+5=9 Combine next smallest: 9+9=18 Continue until root: 57 Codes: E: 00 T: 011 N: 0101 I: 0100 S: 1 Encoded message 001011000100 → Decodes to TENTS. Building the tree: O(n log n) using a priority queue. Encoding/decoding: O(L) where L is message length. Significant space savings compared to fixed-length coding. Simple and efficient for small alphabets. Requires transmitting the code table along with the message. Best suited for static frequency distributions.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/insertion-sor.title: Insertion Sort
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/insertion-sor.description: Insertion Sort is a simple comparison-based sorting algorithm that builds the final sorted array one element at a time by inserting each element into its correct position.Divide the list into a "sorted" and "unsorted" part. Repeatedly take the first element from the unsorted part and insert it into the correct position in the sorted part. Start with the second element (first element is considered sorted). Compare it with elements in the sorted part. Shift larger elements to the right. Insert the current element into its correct position. Repeat until all elements are sorted. A pass is considered complete when an element is sorted into its place. So a third pass will have 4 elements in the "sorted section". The first element and the 3 inserted elements. Time: Best case: (already sorted) Average case: Worst case: Yes (does not change the relative order of equal elements).Efficient for small datasets or nearly sorted arrays; commonly used in practice for small arrays or as part of more complex algorithms. It picks up the next unsorted element then checks the sorted elements from the right. If the sorted element is greater than the unsorted element. Remember the sorted elements index, then move the sorted element 1 index up. If the sorted element is less than the unsorted element or start is reached. Place the unsorted element into the remembered index. Iterative Solutiondef insertionSort(alist): # Define a function called insertionSort that takes a list 'alist' as input. for index in range(1, len(alist)): # Loop through the list starting from the second element (index 1) to the end. # The first element is considered sorted. currentvalue = alist[index] # Store the current element that needs to be inserted into the sorted portion. position = index # Initialize 'position' to the current index. This will track where to insert the element. while position > 0 and alist[position - 1] > currentvalue: # While we haven't reached the start of the list AND # the previous element is greater than the current value: alist[position] = alist[position - 1] # Shift the previous element one position to the right. position = position - 1 # Move the position pointer one step to the left. alist[position] = currentvalue # Insert the current value into its correct position in the sorted portion. # Example usage: alist = [54, 26, 93, 17, 77, 31, 44, 55, 20] # Create a list of unsorted integers. insertionSort(alist) # Call the insertionSort function to sort the list. print(alist) # Print the sorted list. Recursive Solutiondef recInsertionSort(arr, n): # Base case: if the array has 1 element, it's already sorted if n <= 1: return # Sort the first n-1 elements recInsertionSort(arr, n - 1) # Insert the nth element into the correct position in the sorted part currentvalue = arr[n - 1] position = n - 2 # Shift elements that are greater than currentvalue to the right while position >= 0 and arr[position] > currentvalue: arr[position + 1] = arr[position] position = position - 1 # Place currentvalue in its correct position arr[position + 1] = currentvalue # Test the recursive insertion sort alist = [54, 26, 93, 17, 77, 31, 44, 55, 20] recInsertionSort(alist, len(alist)) print(alist) Run

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/kruskal’s-alg.title: Kruskal’s Algorithm
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/kruskal’s-alg.description:
Definition: Kruskal’s Algorithm is a greedy algorithm used to find a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) for a weighted, connected, undirected graph. The MST connects all vertices with the minimum total edge weight and without cycles. Sort all edges by weight in ascending order. Add edges one by one to the MST, skipping any edge that would create a cycle. Continue until all vertices are included. Input: Weighted, connected, undirected graph. Output: Minimum Spanning Tree (n-1 edges). Approach: Greedy (chooses smallest edge first). Complexity: Sorting edges: . Union-Find operations: with efficient disjoint-set structure. Sort all edges from lowest to highest weight. Initialize MST as empty. For each edge in sorted order: If adding the edge does not form a cycle, include it in the MST. Otherwise, discard the edge. Repeat until MST has edges. Network design (minimum cost connections). Road or pipeline construction. Clustering in data analysis. Operates on Graphs and produces a Spanning Tree.
Related to Prim's Algorithm (another MST algorithm). Both Prim’s and Kruskal’s are greedy algorithms.
MST differs from Dijkstra’s Algorithm (shortest path problem).

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/linked-list.h.title: Linked List
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/linked-list.h.description:
Definition: A Linked List is a linear data structure where elements, called nodes, are stored in separate memory locations and connected via pointers. Unlike arrays, linked lists do not require contiguous memory allocation, allowing dynamic resizing during program execution. Dynamic Size: Can grow or shrink during runtime. Non-contiguous Storage: Each node resides in a separate memory location. Sequential Access: Traversal requires moving from one node to the next; no random access. Node: Basic unit containing: Data: The value stored. Pointer(s): Reference(s) to adjacent node(s). Head: Reference to the first node (null if empty). Tail (optional): Reference to the last node for efficient appends. Think of a linked list as a chain: each link holds data and connects to the next. To reach any element, you must traverse sequentially from the start—no shortcuts. Singly Linked List: Each node points to the next; last node points to null.
Doubly Linked List: Nodes have two pointers—next and previous—for bidirectional traversal.
Circular Linked List: Last node links back to the first, forming a loop (can be singly or doubly). Traversal (O(n)): Visit nodes sequentially from head. Insertion: At beginning: O(1) At end: O(n) At position: O(n) Deletion: From beginning: O(1) From end or position: O(n) Searching: O(n) Dynamic memory usage. Efficient insertion/deletion at beginning. No direct access (must traverse). Extra memory for pointers. Links to Related Notes:
Singly Linked List
Doubly Linked List
Circular Linked List

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/linked-lists..title: Linked Lists
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/linked-lists..description: A linked list is a linear data structure where elements (called nodes) are stored in separate objects, each containing a value and a reference (or pointer) to the next node in the sequence.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/merge-sort.ht.title: Merge Sort
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/merge-sort.ht.description: Merge Sort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that recursively splits the list into smaller sublists, sorts them, and then merges the sorted sublists back together. Divide the list into two halves. Recursively sort each half. Merge the two sorted halves into one sorted list. If the list has 1 or 0 elements, it is already sorted. Split the list into two halves. Recursively apply Merge Sort to each half. Merge the two sorted halves by comparing elements and building a new sorted list. Compare the first element in each group, add the smallest one to the new merged group. Repeat untill groups are empty. A pass can be considered on what merge step one is on. Merge into groups of 2 is first pass Merge into groups of 4 is second pass etc. Time: - Best case: O(n log n) - Average case: O(n log n) - Worst case: O(n log n) Yes (preserves the relative order of equal elements).Efficient for large datasets; commonly used in external sorting (e.g., sorting data on disk) and as a foundation for other algorithms.Recursive Solutiondef mergeSort(array,p,r): if p<r: q = (p+r)//2 # r is the point where the array is divided into two subarrays mergeSort(array,p,q) # Sort the two halves mergeSort(array,q+1,r) merge(array, p,q,r) def merge(array, p,q,r): # Create temp arrays L = array[p:q+1] R = array[q+1:r+1] i = j = 0 k = p # Until we reach either end of either L or R, pick larger among elements L and R and place them in the correct position at A[p..r] while i < len(L) and j < len(R): if L[i] <= R[j]: array[k] = L[i] i += 1 else: array[k] = R[j] j += 1 k += 1 while i < len(L): array[k] = L[i] i += 1 k += 1 while j < len(R): array[k] = R[j] j += 1 k += 1 def printList(array): for i in range(len(array)): print(array[i], end=" ") print() # Driver program if __name__ == '__main__': array = [6, 5, 12, 10, 9, 1] mergeSort(array,0,len(array)-1) print("Sorted array is: ") printList(array)

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/prim's-algori.title: Prim's Algorithm
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/prim's-algori.description:
Definition: Prim’s Algorithm is a greedy algorithm used to find a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) for a weighted, connected, undirected graph. The MST is a subset of edges that connects all vertices with the minimum possible total edge weight and without cycles. Start from an arbitrary vertex. Grow the MST by repeatedly adding the smallest-weight edge that connects a vertex in the tree to a vertex outside the tree. Continue until all vertices are included. Input: Weighted, connected, undirected graph. Output: Minimum Spanning Tree (n-1 edges). Approach: Greedy (local optimum → global optimum). Complexity: Using adjacency matrix: . Using min-priority queue (binary heap): Initialize MST with a random starting vertex. Find all edges connecting the tree to vertices not yet in the tree. Select the edge with the minimum weight and add it to the MST. Repeat step 2 and 3 until all vertices are included. Network design (minimum cost wiring). Road construction (minimum total distance). Clustering in machine learning. Operates on Graphs and produces a Spanning Tree.
Related to Kruskal’s Algorithm (another MST algorithm). Both Prim’s and Kruskal’s are greedy algorithms.
MST differs from Dijkstra’s Algorithm (shortest path, not spanning tree).

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/queues.html.title: Queues
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/queues.html.description: A queue is a linear data structure that follows the **First In, First Out (FIFO)** principle. Elements are added at the rear (enqueue) and removed from the front (dequeue).

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/quick-sort.ht.title: Quick Sort
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/quick-sort.ht.description: Quick Sort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that works by selecting a pivot element, partitioning the list into two sublists (elements less than the pivot and elements greater than the pivot), and then recursively sorting the sublists. Choose a pivot element. Partition the list so that: - Elements less than the pivot go to the left. - Elements greater than the pivot go to the right. Recursively apply Quick Sort to the left and right sublists. If the list has 0 or 1 elements, it is already sorted. Select a pivot (commonly first, last, or median element). Partition the list into two sublists based on the pivot. Recursively sort the two sublists. Combine the sorted sublists and pivot into a single sorted list. 1 Pass is considered complete when a pivot is swapped or the pivot logic is complete and a new one should be selected. Time: - Best case: (balanced partitions) - Average case: - Worst case: (when partitions are highly unbalanced) Space: (due to recursion stack) No (equal elements may change their relative order during partitioning).Quick Sort is widely used because of its efficiency and in-place sorting capability. It is often the default sorting algorithm in many programming libraries.def quicksort(arr, left, right): if left < right: partition_pos = partition(arr, left, right) print(partition_pos) quicksort(arr, left,partition_pos-1 ) quicksort(arr, partition_pos+1, right ) def partition(arr, left, right): i = left j = right -1 pivot = arr[right] print(arr, " Pivot:", pivot) while i < j: while i < right and arr[i] < pivot: i += 1 while j > left and arr[j] >= pivot: j -= 1 if i < j: arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i] if arr[i] > pivot: arr[i], arr[right] = arr[right], arr[i] return i a = [-20, -16, 8, -3, 9, 14, 1, -8, 17, 5] quicksort(a, 0, len(a)-1)

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/selection-sor.title: Selection Sort
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/selection-sor.description: Selection Sort is a simple comparison-based sorting algorithm that repeatedly selects the smallest (or largest) element from the unsorted portion of the list and places it at the beginning of the sorted portion.Divide the list into a sorted and unsorted part. Find the minimum element in the unsorted part and swap it with the first unsorted element. Repeat until the entire list is sorted. Start at index 0: Assume the first element is the smallest. Iterate through the rest of the list to find the actual smallest element. Swap the smallest element with the element at index 0. Move to index 1: Now consider the sublist starting at index 1. Find the smallest element in the remaining unsorted portion. Swap it with the element at index 1. Repeat: Continue this process for index 2, 3, … until the second-to-last element. After the last swap, the list is sorted. 1 pass of Selection sort is when the smallest element is swapped in to sorted section. Time: Best case: (already sorted) Average case: Worst case: No (equal elements may change their relative order because of swapping).Simple to implement; useful for small datasets or when memory is extremely limited. Rarely used in practice due to inefficiency compared to more advanced algorithms.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/shell-sort.ht.title: Shell Sort
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/shell-sort.ht.description: Shell Sort is an optimization of Insertion Sort that allows the exchange of far-apart elements. It works by sorting elements at a specific gap and gradually reducing the gap until it becomes 1, at which point the list is fully sorted using Insertion Sort.Start with a large gap between elements and sort sublists formed by these gaps. Reduce the gap step by step, improving the order of the list until the gap is 1. Choose an initial gap (commonly n//2). (The two // means floor division) Sort elements that are gap positions apart using Insertion Sort logic. Reduce the gap (usually by half) and repeat. (n//4, n//8, ...) Continue until the gap is 1, then perform a final pass of Insertion Sort. Note: Minimum 1, floor division can cause floor to 0. 1 pass of shell sort is considered a full iteration involing a gap size. Time: Best case: (already sorted) Average case: Worst case: No (Shell Sort is generally not stable because elements can move far apart).Iterative Solutiondef shellSort(array, n): # Rearrange elements at each n/2, n/4, n/8, ... intervals interval = n // 2 while interval > 0: print('interval', interval) for i in range(interval, n): temp = array[i] j = i while j >= interval and array[j - interval] > temp: array[j] = array[j - interval] j -= interval array[j] = temp print(array) interval //= 2 data = [20, 12, 65, 8, 10, 16, 43, 35, 23, 88, 2, 56, 41, 27, 67, 56] size = len(data) shellSort(data, size) print('Sorted Array in Ascending Order:') print(data)

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/singly-linked.title: Singly Linked List
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/singly-linked.description:
Definition: A Singly Linked List is a type of linked list where each node contains data and a single pointer to the next node in the sequence. The last node points to null (or None), indicating the end of the list. Node Components: Data: Stores the value. Next Pointer: Points to the next node. Head: Reference to the first node. Tail: Not mandatory, but can be maintained for efficient appends. Unidirectional Traversal: Can only move forward from head to tail. Dynamic Size: Can grow or shrink during runtime. No Random Access: Must traverse sequentially to reach a node. Traversal (O(n)): Start at head and follow pointers until null. Insertion: At beginning: O(1) At end: O(n) At position: O(n) Deletion: From beginning: O(1) From end or position: O(n) Searching: O(n) Efficient insertion/deletion at the beginning. Flexible memory usage. Cannot traverse backward. Extra memory for pointers. Slower access compared to arrays. class Node: def __init__(self, data): self.data = data self.next = None class LinkedList: def __init__(self): self.head = None def insert_front(self, new): new_node = Node(new) new_node.next = self.head self.head = new_node def traverse(self): temp = self.head while temp: print(temp.data, end=" ") temp = temp.next RunLinks to Related Notes:
Linked List
Doubly Linked List
Circular Linked List

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/stacks.html.title: Stacks
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/stacks.html.description: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the **Last In, First Out (LIFO)** principle. Elements are added (pushed) and removed (popped) only from the top of the stack.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/time-complexi.title: Time complexity
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/time-complexi.description: Time complexity is a measure of the **amount of time** an algorithm takes to run as a function of the **size of its input (n)**. It describes how the runtime grows with increasing input, helping to evaluate the efficiency of algorithms.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/tree.html.title: Tree
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/tree.html.description:
A tree is a non-linear, hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes connected by edges. Each node holds a value and may have child nodes. Trees are directed (links go one way) and acyclic (no cycles). The topmost node is called the root; nodes with no children are leaves. Root: The first node; every tree has exactly one root. Node: Basic element containing data. Edge: Connection between two nodes. Leaf Node: Node with no children. Parent & Child: Parent has branches to children; all nodes except root are children. Sibling: Nodes sharing the same parent. Internal Node: Node with at least one child. External Node: Node with no children (leaf). Size: Number of nodes in the tree. Degree: Number of children of a node; degree of tree = max degree among nodes. Level/Depth: Distance from root (root = level 0) to lowest level. Height: Length of the longest path from root to any leaf. Subtree: Each child node forms a subtree recursively. Binary Tree: Each node has at most two children (left and right). Full Binary Tree: Every node has either 0 or 2 children. Perfect Binary Tree: All levels are filled. Complete Binary Tree: All levels are filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right.
Binary search trees (BST): Left subtree contains values less than parent. Right subtree contains values greater than parent. Enables efficient search, insertion, and deletion. Insertion: Add a node as a leaf or mid-tree (update parent-child links). Deletion: Case 1: Remove leaf node. Case 2: Remove node with one child (replace with child). Case 3: Remove node with two children (replace with inorder successor). Search: General tree: O(n). Balanced BST: O(log n).
Traversal: Pre-order: Root → Left → Right In-order: Left → Root → Right Post-order: Left → Right → Root Level-order: Breadth-first traversal. BST (Balanced): Search, Insert, Delete: O(log n) BST (Unbalanced): Worst case: O(n) AVL Tree: Self-balancing BST; maintains height balance using rotations. Balance Factor (BF) = height(left) − height(right) ∈ {−1, 0, 1}. Rotations: Single (Left/Right), Double (Left-Right/Right-Left). Hierarchical data representation (e.g., file systems). Efficient searching and sorting (BST, AVL). Priority queues (heaps). Expression parsing.

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/tree-reconstr.title: Tree Reconstruction
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/tree-reconstr.description: Methods to reconstruct a Binary Tree or Binary Search Tree based on Pre-/In-/Post-order arrays

07-stikkord-samling/dat200/tree-traversa.title: Tree traversal algorithms
07-stikkord-samling/dat200/tree-traversa.description: Tree traversal refers to the process of visiting each node in a tree data structure in a specific order. Traversal algorithms define how nodes are accessed and processed, typically in binary trees or binary search trees.

info_showDefaultFolderIcons.description: Show a default icon of a folder for every folder in the tree

info_showDefaultFileIcons.description: Show a default icon of a file for every file in the tree

info_defaultFolderIcon.description: The icon to use for folders. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultFileIcon.description: The icon to use for files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultMediaIcon.description: The icon to use for media files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_exposeStartingPath.description: Whether or not to show the current file in the file tree when the page is first loaded

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_minCollapseDepth.description: Only allow outline items to be collapsed if they are at least this many levels deep in the tree.

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site

bodyClasses: publish css-settings-manager styled-scrollbars show-inline-title show-ribbon is-focused
